cards.title: Magadha
cards.subtitle: Cradle of Empires
cards.description: An ancient kingdom centred on the Ganges plain in present-day Bihar, Magadha rose to dominance from around the 6th century BCE. Its fertile land, iron deposits, and strong capitals at Rajagriha and later Pataliputra made it the seedbed of India's first great empires.

cards.title: Bimbisara
cards.subtitle: Founder of Magadhan Power
cards.description: A king of the Haryanka dynasty who ruled Magadha in the 6th century BCE, Bimbisara expanded his realm through marriage alliances and conquest, including the annexation of Anga. A contemporary of the Buddha and Mahavira, he is remembered as an early patron of both traditions.

cards.title: Ajatashatru
cards.subtitle: Conqueror of the Vajjis
cards.description: Son and successor of Bimbisara, Ajatashatru ruled Magadha in the 5th century BCE and defeated the Vajji confederacy of Vaishali after a long campaign. He fortified the village of Pataligrama, which would later grow into the great city of Pataliputra.

cards.title: The Nandas
cards.subtitle: Lords of the First Great Army
cards.description: The Nanda dynasty ruled Magadha in the 4th century BCE and built an unprecedentedly large standing army and a vast treasury. Their power, described by Greek sources at the time of Alexander, was eventually overthrown by Chandragupta Maurya and Chanakya.

cards.title: Chanakya
cards.subtitle: The Kingmaker
cards.description: Also known as Kautilya, Chanakya was the scholar and strategist who guided Chandragupta Maurya to overthrow the Nandas and found the Mauryan Empire. He is traditionally credited with the Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft, economics, and warfare.

cards.title: Chandragupta Maurya
cards.subtitle: Founder of the Mauryas
cards.description: Founder of the Mauryan Empire around 321 BCE, Chandragupta unified much of the Indian subcontinent and pushed back the successors of Alexander in the northwest. Tradition holds that he later abdicated and embraced Jainism, ending his life as an ascetic in the south.

cards.title: Maurya Empire
cards.subtitle: India's First Great Empire
cards.description: The Mauryan Empire, founded by Chandragupta Maurya around 321 BCE, became the first state to unify most of the Indian subcontinent. Governed from Pataliputra with an elaborate bureaucracy, it reached its zenith under Ashoka before declining in the 2nd century BCE.

cards.title: Pataliputra
cards.subtitle: Capital of Empires
cards.description: Situated near modern Patna at the confluence of major rivers, Pataliputra served as the capital of the Magadhan, Mauryan, and later Gupta states. The Greek envoy Megasthenes described it as one of the largest and most magnificent cities of the ancient world.

cards.title: The Arthashastra
cards.subtitle: Manual of Statecraft
cards.description: An ancient Indian treatise on statecraft, economics, diplomacy, and military strategy, traditionally attributed to Kautilya (Chanakya). Rediscovered in the early twentieth century, it offers a detailed and pragmatic vision of how a kingdom should be administered.

cards.title: Bindusara
cards.subtitle: The Amitraghata
cards.description: Son of Chandragupta Maurya, Bindusara ruled the Mauryan Empire in the early 3rd century BCE and maintained its vast extent. Greek sources call him Amitrochates, and he kept up diplomatic contact with the Hellenistic kingdoms to the west.

cards.title: Ashoka
cards.subtitle: The Great Mauryan
cards.description: The third Mauryan emperor, Ashoka ruled in the 3rd century BCE and, after the bloody conquest of Kalinga, embraced Buddhism and a policy of dhamma. He spread his moral edicts on rocks and pillars across the subcontinent and sent missions abroad.

cards.title: Kalinga War
cards.subtitle: The War That Changed a King
cards.description: Fought around 261 BCE between the Mauryan emperor Ashoka and the kingdom of Kalinga in present-day Odisha, this campaign was enormously destructive. Ashoka's own edicts record his remorse at the slaughter, marking his turn toward Buddhism and non-violence.

cards.title: Ashoka's Pillars
cards.subtitle: Edicts in Stone
cards.description: Polished sandstone columns erected across the Mauryan realm, many crowned with animal capitals and inscribed with Ashoka's edicts on dhamma. The Lion Capital of Sarnath, atop one such pillar, was later adopted as the State Emblem of independent India.

cards.title: Battle of Hydaspes
cards.subtitle: Porus Against Alexander
cards.description: Fought in 326 BCE on the banks of the Jhelum river, this battle pitted the Indian king Porus against Alexander of Macedon. Though Alexander prevailed, the fierce resistance and the war elephants of Porus made it one of his costliest victories, and he reinstated Porus as a ruler.

cards.title: Kanishka
cards.subtitle: The Great Kushan
cards.description: The most renowned ruler of the Kushan Empire, Kanishka reigned around the 2nd century CE over a realm spanning Central Asia and northern India. A patron of Buddhism, he is associated with the Fourth Buddhist Council and the flourishing of Gandharan art.

cards.title: The Satavahanas
cards.subtitle: Masters of the Deccan
cards.description: The Satavahana dynasty ruled the Deccan and parts of central India for several centuries around the turn of the Common Era. Known for patronage of trade, Buddhism, and Brahmanical traditions, rulers such as Gautamiputra Satakarni resisted the Western Kshatrapas.

cards.title: Gupta Empire
cards.subtitle: A Classical Golden Age
cards.description: Flourishing from the 4th to 6th centuries CE, the Gupta Empire presided over a celebrated era of achievement in mathematics, astronomy, literature, and art. Centred in northern India, it is often described as a classical golden age of Indian civilization.

cards.title: Chandragupta I
cards.subtitle: Founder of Gupta Glory
cards.description: The ruler who raised the Gupta family to imperial status in the early 4th century CE, Chandragupta I strengthened his position through a marriage alliance with the Lichchhavis. He adopted the grand title Maharajadhiraja, king of great kings.

cards.title: Samudragupta
cards.subtitle: The Indian Napoleon
cards.description: A 4th-century Gupta emperor celebrated for wide-ranging military campaigns recorded in the Allahabad pillar inscription. A patron of the arts and himself a musician, he extended Gupta influence across much of the subcontinent through conquest and alliance.

cards.title: Chandragupta II Vikramaditya
cards.subtitle: Sun of Valour
cards.description: A Gupta emperor of the late 4th and early 5th centuries CE, Chandragupta II expanded the empire by defeating the Western Kshatrapas and took the title Vikramaditya. His court is traditionally associated with great poets and scholars of the classical age.

cards.title: Iron Pillar of Delhi
cards.subtitle: The Rustless Wonder
cards.description: A wrought-iron column now standing in the Qutb complex in Delhi, dating to the Gupta era and bearing a Sanskrit inscription honouring a king named Chandra. Famous for resisting corrosion for over a millennium, it is a testament to early Indian metallurgy.

cards.title: Harsha
cards.subtitle: Lord of the North
cards.description: Ruling from Kannauj in the 7th century CE, Harshavardhana built a large kingdom across northern India and was a noted patron of learning and the arts. His reign is richly documented by the poet Banabhatta and the Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang.

cards.title: Pulakeshin II
cards.subtitle: The Chalukya Champion
cards.description: The greatest ruler of the Chalukya dynasty of Badami, Pulakeshin II reigned in the 7th century CE and famously halted Harsha's southern advance on the Narmada. His court received an embassy from the Sasanian Persian king, attesting to his fame.

cards.title: The Pallavas
cards.subtitle: Architects of the South
cards.description: Ruling from Kanchipuram between roughly the 4th and 9th centuries CE, the Pallavas were great patrons of temple architecture and Tamil and Sanskrit learning. Their monuments at Mahabalipuram, including the shore temple and rock-cut shrines, are renowned.

cards.title: Kanchipuram
cards.subtitle: City of Temples
cards.description: An ancient city in present-day Tamil Nadu that served as the capital of the Pallavas and a major centre of learning, trade, and temple-building. Long revered as one of India's holy cities, it is renowned for its Hindu temples and silk weaving.

cards.title: Mihira Bhoja
cards.subtitle: The Pratihara Powerhouse
cards.description: A 9th-century ruler of the Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty, Mihira Bhoja held a vast realm in northern India and was a devotee of Vishnu. The Pratiharas under him were one of the three great powers contending for control of Kannauj.

cards.title: The Rashtrakutas
cards.subtitle: Builders of Ellora
cards.description: A powerful Deccan dynasty from the 8th to 10th centuries CE, the Rashtrakutas contended for control of northern India and patronised art and literature in several languages. They are especially celebrated for the rock-cut Kailasa temple at Ellora.

cards.title: The Cheras
cards.subtitle: Spice Coast Kings
cards.description: An ancient Tamil dynasty ruling the Malabar coast of present-day Kerala, the Cheras feature prominently in early Sangam literature. Their control of the pepper and spice trade linked them to merchants from Rome, Arabia, and beyond.

cards.title: The Pandyas
cards.subtitle: Pearls of the Tamil South
cards.description: One of the three ancient Tamil dynasties, the Pandyas ruled the far south from Madurai across many centuries. Renowned for the pearl fishery, Sangam-era patronage of poetry, and the great temples of their later medieval revival.

cards.title: Chola Empire
cards.subtitle: Sovereigns of the Seas
cards.description: A Tamil dynasty whose medieval empire, at its height in the 10th to 12th centuries CE, dominated southern India and reached across the seas to Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia. The Cholas are celebrated for their administration, bronzes, and temple-building.

cards.title: Rajaraja Chola I
cards.subtitle: The Great Chola
cards.description: One of the greatest Chola emperors, Rajaraja I reigned around the turn of the 11th century CE and expanded the empire across southern India and into Sri Lanka. He commissioned the magnificent Brihadisvara temple at Thanjavur.

cards.title: Brihadisvara Temple
cards.subtitle: Temple of the Great Chola
cards.description: A monumental Shiva temple at Thanjavur built by Rajaraja Chola I and completed around 1010 CE, crowned by a towering vimana. A masterpiece of Chola architecture and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, it stands among the great temples of India.

cards.title: Rajendra Chola I
cards.subtitle: Lord of the Ganges
cards.description: Son of Rajaraja I, Rajendra Chola I extended the empire to its greatest reach in the 11th century CE, leading expeditions to the Ganges and naval campaigns to Srivijaya in Southeast Asia. He founded a new capital, Gangaikonda Cholapuram.

cards.title: Thanjavur
cards.subtitle: Heart of the Chola Realm
cards.description: The capital of the medieval Cholas in the Kaveri delta of Tamil Nadu, Thanjavur was a centre of administration, temple-building, and the arts. It remained a seat of power under later dynasties, including the Nayaks and Marathas.

cards.title: The Hoysalas
cards.subtitle: Sculptors in Stone
cards.description: A dynasty ruling parts of the southern Deccan from roughly the 11th to 14th centuries CE, the Hoysalas are famed for their intricately carved star-shaped temples at Belur, Halebidu, and Somanathapura. Their realm fell amid the upheavals of the 14th century.

cards.title: The Kakatiyas
cards.subtitle: Rulers of Warangal
cards.description: A dynasty centred on Warangal in present-day Telangana, the Kakatiyas rose to prominence between the 12th and 14th centuries CE. They are remembered for irrigation tanks, fortifications, and notable temples before falling to the Delhi Sultanate.

cards.title: Rani Rudrama Devi
cards.subtitle: The Warrior Queen of Warangal
cards.description: One of the few women to rule a major Indian kingdom in her own right, Rudrama Devi governed the Kakatiya realm in the 13th century CE. She defended her territory against rivals and was praised by the Venetian traveller Marco Polo.

cards.title: Sun Temple of Konark
cards.subtitle: Chariot of the Sun
cards.description: A 13th-century CE temple in present-day Odisha built by the Eastern Ganga ruler Narasimhadeva I, conceived as a colossal stone chariot of the sun god Surya. A masterpiece of temple architecture, it is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

cards.title: Prithviraj Chauhan
cards.subtitle: The Last Hindu Emperor of Delhi
cards.description: A Chahamana (Chauhan) king of Ajmer and Delhi in the late 12th century CE, Prithviraj III is celebrated in legend and the epic Prithviraj Raso. He defeated Muhammad of Ghor at the First Battle of Tarain but was defeated and slain after the second.

cards.title: Battle of Tarain
cards.subtitle: The Turning of the Tide
cards.description: Two battles fought near Tarain in present-day Haryana in 1191 and 1192 CE between Prithviraj Chauhan and Muhammad of Ghor. The Ghurid victory in the second battle opened northern India to Turkish rule and the founding of the Delhi Sultanate.

cards.title: Delhi Sultanate
cards.subtitle: Five Dynasties of Delhi
cards.description: A Muslim state based in Delhi that ruled large parts of the Indian subcontinent from 1206 to 1526 CE through five successive dynasties. It reshaped the political and cultural landscape of medieval India before being supplanted by the Mughals.

cards.title: Qutb-ud-din Aibak
cards.subtitle: The Slave Who Became Sultan
cards.description: A former slave general of Muhammad of Ghor, Qutb-ud-din Aibak became the first Sultan of Delhi in 1206 CE and founded the Mamluk or Slave dynasty. He began construction of the Qutb Minar in Delhi.

cards.title: Iltutmish
cards.subtitle: Consolidator of the Sultanate
cards.description: The third Sultan of Delhi, Iltutmish reigned in the early 13th century CE and is regarded as the true consolidator of the Delhi Sultanate. He organised its administration, introduced the silver tanka coin, and completed the Qutb Minar.

cards.title: Qutb Minar
cards.subtitle: Tower of Victory
cards.description: A soaring brick-and-stone minaret in Delhi begun by Qutb-ud-din Aibak around 1199 CE and completed by Iltutmish. The tallest such tower in India, it stands within a complex that marks the early architecture of the Delhi Sultanate.

cards.title: Razia Sultana
cards.subtitle: The Sultan Who Was a Queen
cards.description: Daughter of Iltutmish, Razia ruled the Delhi Sultanate from 1236 to 1240 CE as the first and only woman to sit on the throne of Delhi. Able and assertive, she faced opposition from the nobility and was eventually overthrown.

cards.title: Balban
cards.subtitle: The Iron Sultan
cards.description: A powerful Sultan of Delhi of the Mamluk dynasty in the later 13th century CE, Balban restored royal authority after a period of weakness. He enforced strict court etiquette and crushed internal revolts and external threats with a firm hand.

cards.title: Alauddin Khalji
cards.subtitle: The Market Reformer
cards.description: A Sultan of the Khalji dynasty who ruled Delhi from 1296 to 1316 CE, Alauddin repelled Mongol invasions and extended the Sultanate deep into the south. He is known for sweeping market price controls and a powerful standing army.

cards.title: Rani Padmini
cards.subtitle: The Legend of Chittor
cards.description: Padmini, or Padmavati, is the legendary queen of Chittor associated with the siege of the fort by Alauddin Khalji around 1303 CE. Immortalised in Malik Muhammad Jayasi's later poem Padmavat, her story is woven from history and legend.

cards.title: Muhammad bin Tughlaq
cards.subtitle: The Ambitious Sultan
cards.description: A Sultan of the Tughlaq dynasty who ruled Delhi from 1325 to 1351 CE, known for bold and ill-fated schemes. His attempt to shift the capital to Daulatabad and his token currency experiment are famous episodes of medieval Indian history.

cards.title: Firuz Shah Tughlaq
cards.subtitle: The Builder Sultan
cards.description: A Sultan of the Tughlaq dynasty who reigned from 1351 to 1388 CE, Firuz Shah is remembered for public works such as canals, towns, and the repair of monuments. He moved two of Ashoka's pillars to Delhi and founded the city of Firozabad.

cards.title: Bahmani Sultanate
cards.subtitle: The First Deccan Sultanate
cards.description: Founded in 1347 CE, the Bahmani Sultanate was the first independent Muslim kingdom of the Deccan, ruling from Gulbarga and later Bidar. It contended with Vijayanagara before fragmenting into the five Deccan sultanates.

cards.title: Vijayanagara Empire
cards.subtitle: The City of Victory
cards.description: A powerful southern empire founded in 1336 CE that dominated peninsular India for over two centuries from its capital at Hampi. Vijayanagara was a great centre of temple-building, trade, and Hindu culture before its defeat at Talikota.

cards.title: Harihara and Bukka
cards.subtitle: Founders of Vijayanagara
cards.description: Two brothers who founded the Vijayanagara Empire and its first Sangama dynasty in 1336 CE on the southern bank of the Tungabhadra. Their new capital grew into one of the largest and richest cities of its age.

cards.title: Hampi
cards.subtitle: City of Victory
cards.description: The capital of the Vijayanagara Empire, set among striking granite boulders on the Tungabhadra river in present-day Karnataka. Its extensive ruins, including the Virupaksha temple and Vittala complex, are a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

cards.title: Krishnadevaraya
cards.subtitle: The Vijayanagara Zenith
cards.description: The most celebrated emperor of Vijayanagara, Krishnadevaraya reigned from 1509 to 1529 CE and led the empire to its political and cultural peak. A patron of Telugu and Sanskrit literature, he was himself an accomplished poet.

cards.title: Battle of Talikota
cards.subtitle: The Fall of Vijayanagara
cards.description: Fought in 1565 CE between Vijayanagara and an alliance of Deccan sultanates, this battle ended in a crushing defeat for the empire. The victorious armies sacked the great capital of Hampi, from which Vijayanagara never fully recovered.

cards.title: Golconda
cards.subtitle: Fortress of Diamonds
cards.description: A fortified citadel near Hyderabad and seat of the Qutb Shahi dynasty of Golconda from the 16th century. Famed for its diamond trade and remarkable acoustics, the fort eventually fell to the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb in 1687.

cards.title: Ahom Kingdom
cards.subtitle: Guardians of the Brahmaputra
cards.description: A kingdom that ruled the Brahmaputra valley of Assam for some six centuries from the 13th century CE. The Ahoms successfully resisted repeated Mughal invasions and developed a distinctive administration and culture in the northeast.

cards.title: Babur
cards.subtitle: Founder of the Mughals
cards.description: A Timurid prince from Central Asia, Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi at Panipat in 1526 CE and founded the Mughal Empire in India. A gifted soldier and writer, he left a vivid memoir, the Baburnama, describing his conquests and observations.

cards.title: Ibrahim Lodi
cards.subtitle: The Last Sultan of Delhi
cards.description: The last Sultan of the Lodi dynasty and of the Delhi Sultanate, Ibrahim Lodi was defeated and killed by Babur at the First Battle of Panipat in 1526 CE. His death cleared the way for Mughal rule in northern India.

cards.title: First Battle of Panipat
cards.subtitle: Dawn of the Mughals
cards.description: Fought in 1526 CE between Babur and Ibrahim Lodi, this battle saw Babur's outnumbered forces triumph through field artillery and mobile tactics. The victory ended the Delhi Sultanate and established the Mughal Empire in India.

cards.title: Rana Sanga
cards.subtitle: The Rajput Champion
cards.description: Maharana Sangram Singh of Mewar, known as Rana Sanga, was a formidable Rajput ruler of the early 16th century CE. He led a confederacy of Rajput states against Babur at the Battle of Khanwa in 1527, where he was defeated.

cards.title: Mughal Empire
cards.subtitle: The Grand Mughals
cards.description: A vast empire founded by Babur in 1526 CE that, at its height, ruled most of the Indian subcontinent. Renowned for centralised administration, syncretic culture, and magnificent art and architecture, it endured in some form until 1857.

cards.title: Humayun
cards.subtitle: The Fortune-Tossed Emperor
cards.description: The second Mughal emperor, Humayun lost his throne to Sher Shah Suri and spent years in exile in Persia before reclaiming Delhi in 1555 CE. His early death the following year passed the empire to his young son Akbar.

cards.title: Sher Shah Suri
cards.subtitle: The Administrator King
cards.description: An Afghan ruler who defeated Humayun and founded the Sur dynasty, ruling northern India from 1540 to 1545 CE. A brilliant administrator, he reformed revenue and currency, introduced the rupiya, and built the Grand Trunk Road network.

cards.title: Akbar
cards.subtitle: Akbar the Great
cards.description: The third Mughal emperor, Akbar reigned from 1556 to 1605 CE and greatly expanded and consolidated the empire. Known for religious tolerance, administrative reform, and the experimental faith Din-i-Ilahi, he is often called Akbar the Great.

cards.title: Second Battle of Panipat
cards.subtitle: Akbar Secures the Throne
cards.description: Fought in 1556 CE between the forces of the young Akbar, led by Bairam Khan, and Hemu, a Hindu general who had seized Delhi. The Mughal victory restored the dynasty's hold on northern India early in Akbar's reign.

cards.title: Fatehpur Sikri
cards.subtitle: Akbar's Abandoned Capital
cards.description: A planned city built by Akbar near Agra in the 1570s to serve as his capital, blending Persian and Indian architectural styles. Renowned for the Buland Darwaza and Jama Masjid, it was abandoned after a short time, reportedly due to water scarcity.

cards.title: Maharana Pratap
cards.subtitle: The Hero of Mewar
cards.description: The Sisodia Rajput ruler of Mewar in the late 16th century CE, Maharana Pratap resisted Mughal expansion under Akbar. Celebrated for his defiance at the Battle of Haldighati and his loyal steed Chetak, he became an enduring symbol of resistance.

cards.title: Battle of Haldighati
cards.subtitle: The Stand of Mewar
cards.description: Fought in 1576 CE between Maharana Pratap of Mewar and the Mughal forces of Akbar led by Man Singh. Though tactically inconclusive and costly for Mewar, the battle became legendary as a symbol of Rajput defiance.

cards.title: Jahangir
cards.subtitle: The Connoisseur Emperor
cards.description: The fourth Mughal emperor, Jahangir reigned from 1605 to 1627 CE and was a noted patron of painting and the arts. His reign saw growing European trade contact, including the embassy of Sir Thomas Roe from England.

cards.title: Nur Jahan
cards.subtitle: The Power Behind the Throne
cards.description: The influential consort of the emperor Jahangir, Nur Jahan wielded remarkable political power in the early 17th century CE. She issued coins in her name and effectively guided court affairs, a rare role for a woman of the Mughal court.

cards.title: Shah Jahan
cards.subtitle: Builder of the Taj
cards.description: The fifth Mughal emperor, Shah Jahan reigned from 1628 to 1658 CE during a period of architectural splendour. He built the Taj Mahal in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal and moved the capital to his new city of Shahjahanabad in Delhi.

cards.title: Taj Mahal
cards.subtitle: A Monument to Love
cards.description: A white marble mausoleum at Agra built by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan for his wife Mumtaz Mahal, completed around 1653 CE. A masterpiece of Mughal architecture and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, it is among the most celebrated buildings in the world.

cards.title: The Peacock Throne
cards.subtitle: Seat of Mughal Splendour
cards.description: A jewelled ceremonial throne commissioned by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan, famed for its lavish use of gold and gems. It was carried off by the Persian invader Nadir Shah in 1739, along with much of the imperial treasure.

cards.title: Agra
cards.subtitle: City of the Mughals
cards.description: A city on the Yamuna that served as a principal capital of the Mughal Empire, especially under Akbar and Shah Jahan. It is home to the Agra Fort and the Taj Mahal, two of the empire's most celebrated monuments.

cards.title: Aurangzeb
cards.subtitle: The Last Great Mughal
cards.description: The sixth Mughal emperor, Aurangzeb reigned from 1658 to 1707 CE and extended the empire to its greatest territorial extent. His long campaigns in the Deccan and centralising rule marked the empire's final era of dominance before its decline.

cards.title: Shivaji
cards.subtitle: Founder of the Marathas
cards.description: The founder of the Maratha kingdom in the 17th century CE, Shivaji Bhonsle built a state in western India through guerrilla warfare and naval power against the Mughals and the Deccan sultanates. He was crowned Chhatrapati at Raigad in 1674.

cards.title: Maratha Empire
cards.subtitle: The Hindavi Swarajya
cards.description: A power founded by Shivaji in the 17th century CE that grew into a vast confederacy dominating much of India in the 18th century. Led later by the Peshwas and great chiefs, the Marathas were a principal rival of the Mughals and the British.

cards.title: Raigad Fort
cards.subtitle: Throne of the Marathas
cards.description: A hill fortress in the Sahyadri range of present-day Maharashtra that served as the capital of Shivaji's Maratha kingdom. It was here that Shivaji was crowned Chhatrapati in 1674, and the fort remains closely tied to Maratha identity.

cards.title: Sambhaji
cards.subtitle: The Warrior Successor
cards.description: The eldest son and successor of Shivaji, Sambhaji led the Maratha kingdom from 1681 CE in continuous war against the Mughals under Aurangzeb. Captured and executed in 1689, his resistance became part of Maratha lore.

cards.title: Baji Rao I
cards.subtitle: The Unbeaten Peshwa
cards.description: The Peshwa, or prime minister, of the Maratha Empire from 1720 to 1740 CE, Baji Rao I led rapid cavalry campaigns that expanded Maratha power across northern India. He is remembered as a brilliant general who is said never to have lost a battle.

cards.title: Ahilyabai Holkar
cards.subtitle: The Philosopher Queen
cards.description: The Holkar queen who ruled the Malwa realm from Maheshwar in the later 18th century CE, Ahilyabai is renowned for just and pious governance. She funded the building and repair of temples, ghats, and rest houses across India.

cards.title: Mahadji Scindia
cards.subtitle: The Maratha Statesman
cards.description: A leading Maratha chief and statesman of the later 18th century CE, Mahadji Scindia rebuilt Maratha power in the north after the setback at Panipat. He became the dominant power at the Mughal court in Delhi and modernised his army on European lines.

cards.title: Third Battle of Panipat
cards.subtitle: The Great Reckoning
cards.description: Fought in 1761 CE between the Maratha Empire and the Afghan forces of Ahmad Shah Durrani, this was one of the largest battles of the 18th century. The Maratha defeat caused heavy losses and temporarily checked their expansion in the north.

cards.title: Rani Durgavati
cards.subtitle: The Gond Queen
cards.description: The queen of the Gondwana kingdom of Garha-Mandla, Rani Durgavati ruled in the 16th century CE and resisted Mughal expansion. She died defending her realm against the forces of Akbar in 1564, choosing death over capture.

cards.title: Chittorgarh
cards.subtitle: Citadel of the Rajputs
cards.description: A great hill fort in present-day Rajasthan that was long the capital of the Sisodia rulers of Mewar. Site of famous sieges and acts of jauhar, it stands as one of the most storied fortresses of Rajput history.

cards.title: Guru Gobind Singh
cards.subtitle: The Tenth Guru
cards.description: The tenth Sikh Guru, who led the community in the late 17th and early 18th centuries CE and founded the Khalsa in 1699. A warrior, poet, and spiritual leader, he shaped the martial and religious identity of the Sikh tradition.

cards.title: Sikh Empire
cards.subtitle: Sovereigns of the Punjab
cards.description: A state founded by Maharaja Ranjit Singh in 1799 CE that unified the Sikh misls and ruled the Punjab and surrounding regions. Known for a strong, modernised army, it was the last major Indian power to fall to the British, in 1849.

cards.title: Banda Singh Bahadur
cards.subtitle: The First Sikh Sovereign
cards.description: A Sikh military commander who, in the early 18th century CE, led campaigns against the Mughal authorities in the Punjab after the death of Guru Gobind Singh. He established Sikh rule in parts of the region before his capture and execution in 1716.

cards.title: Maharaja Ranjit Singh
cards.subtitle: The Lion of Punjab
cards.description: The founder and Maharaja of the Sikh Empire, Ranjit Singh unified the Punjab in the early 19th century CE and built a powerful, modern army. Known for religious tolerance and able governance, he kept the British east of the Sutlej during his lifetime.

cards.title: The Koh-i-Noor
cards.subtitle: Mountain of Light
cards.description: One of the most famous diamonds in the world, the Koh-i-Noor passed through the hands of many dynasties, including the Mughals, Persians, Afghans, and the Sikh court. It was surrendered to the British Crown after the annexation of Punjab in 1849.

cards.title: Nadir Shah's Invasion
cards.subtitle: The Sack of Delhi
cards.description: In 1739 CE the Persian ruler Nadir Shah invaded the declining Mughal Empire and defeated its army at Karnal. He sacked Delhi and carried off immense treasure, including the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor, accelerating Mughal decline.

cards.title: Hyder Ali
cards.subtitle: The Soldier of Mysore
cards.description: A military commander who rose to become the de facto ruler of the Kingdom of Mysore in the later 18th century CE. He built a strong army, employed rockets in warfare, and fought the British in the first two Anglo-Mysore Wars.

cards.title: Tipu Sultan
cards.subtitle: The Tiger of Mysore
cards.description: The ruler of Mysore from 1782 CE and son of Hyder Ali, Tipu Sultan resisted British expansion in southern India and pioneered the military use of iron-cased rockets. He died defending his capital Srirangapatna in 1799.

cards.title: Marthanda Varma
cards.subtitle: Maker of Travancore
cards.description: The ruler who consolidated the kingdom of Travancore in the 18th century CE, Marthanda Varma is credited with building a strong, centralised state. He defeated the Dutch East India Company at the Battle of Colachel in 1741.

cards.title: Lachit Borphukan
cards.subtitle: Hero of the Brahmaputra
cards.description: An Ahom military commander who led the defence of Assam against the Mughals in the 17th century CE. He is celebrated for the decisive naval victory at the Battle of Saraighat in 1671, which halted Mughal advance into the region.

cards.title: Siraj-ud-Daulah
cards.subtitle: The Last Nawab of Bengal
cards.description: The Nawab of Bengal who confronted the rising power of the British East India Company in the mid-18th century CE. His defeat at the Battle of Plassey in 1757 marked a decisive step in the Company's rise to political power in India.

cards.title: Battle of Plassey
cards.subtitle: The Company Ascendant
cards.description: Fought in 1757 CE between the British East India Company under Robert Clive and Siraj-ud-Daulah, Nawab of Bengal. Won largely through the defection of the Nawab's commander Mir Jafar, it gave the Company control of Bengal's wealth and resources.

cards.title: Nizam of Hyderabad
cards.subtitle: Lord of the Deccan
cards.description: The title of the rulers of the princely state of Hyderabad, founded by Asaf Jah I, a Mughal governor who became independent in the early 18th century CE. The Asaf Jahi dynasty governed a vast Deccan domain for over two centuries.
